```{r}
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Each point is a decile of the predicted score distribution. Mean
observed first-release rate on the vertical axis; mean predicted probability on the
horizontal axis. Point size is proportional to the number of facility-years in the
decile. The dashed line is the 45-degree reference. 3,929,425 facility-years;
27,455 first-release events.
\end{flushleft}

@fig-cell-risk shows the direction and shape of the predicted risk gradients by
aggregating out-of-sample predictions within each rate cell defined by wall
construction type and tank age group. Each cell mean reflects the model-estimated
risk of the facilities that actually belong to that cell, adjusted for the full set
of observable covariates and predicted on data the model did not see during
training. For single-walled facilities the predicted release rate rises steeply
with age, from 5.3 per thousand facility-years in the youngest age group to more
than 12 per thousand from the 18-20 year group onward, with a compression in the
three-to-five year range that likely reflects the early-period composition of the
stock. Double-walled facilities show a substantially flatter profile, remaining
near 3.5 to 5 per thousand across most age groups. The gap between wall types
widens with age: by the 15-17 year group single-walled facilities carry a
predicted rate of 10.1 per thousand facility-years compared to 4.5 for
double-walled facilities, a differential of 5.6 per thousand, and the gap reaches
6.9 per thousand by the 18-20 year group. These gradients are estimated from
pre-treatment data without imposing any structure on their shape, and they
correspond closely to the rate differentials in the Mid-Continent schedule
documented in @sec-premium.
```{r}
#| label: fig-cell-risk
#| fig-cap: "Out-of-sample predicted first-release rate by tank age group and wall construction type. Each point is the average cross-validated predicted probability within the cell, reported per 1,000 facility-years."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Figures", "Figure_CV_CellRisk.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Average out-of-sample predicted probability within each
(age group, wall type) cell from the saturated penalized logistic regression.
Single-walled (solid, orange) and double-walled (dashed, blue). Reported per
1,000 facility-years. Predictions are recalibrated to the original event-rate
scale prior to aggregation.
\end{flushleft}

@fig-cell-gof confirms that the model-implied cell rates closely recover the
empirical rates observed in the data. Each of the eighteen rate cells is plotted
against its corresponding observed mean release rate. The points lie along the
45-degree line across the full range of cell risk, from roughly three per thousand
at the low end to thirteen per thousand at the high end, with no systematic bias
in either direction. The largest departure between predicted and observed rates
occurs among the youngest double-walled facilities, where the model predicts 7.1
per thousand against an observed rate of 9.4. This discrepancy likely reflects
selection into double-walled construction during the early phase of EPA mandate
compliance, when facilities facing the greatest environmental liability were the
first to upgrade. The remaining seventeen cells show prediction errors of less
than one per thousand, and across all cells the mean absolute deviation between
predicted and observed rates is 0.5 per thousand. This cell-level goodness of fit
is the appropriate standard for an actuarial application: the model must not only
rank facilities correctly in aggregate but assign probabilities that match the
realized rates within the specific combinations of observable characteristics that
define the insurer's rate schedule.
```{r}
#| label: fig-cell-gof
#| fig-cap: "Cell-level goodness of fit: out-of-sample predicted first-release rate against observed rate, by wall type and age group. Each point is one of eighteen rate cells. The dashed line is the 45-degree reference."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Figures", "Figure_CV_CellRisk_GoF.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Each point is one of eighteen (age group, wall type) cells.
Observed first-release rate on the horizontal axis; out-of-sample predicted rate
on the vertical axis. Point size is proportional to the number of facility-years
in the cell. Single-walled cells shown as circles (orange); double-walled as
triangles (blue). The dashed line is the 45-degree reference.
\end{flushleft}



## Remediation Costs Also Scale with Observable Risk {#sec-risk-costs}

For risk-based pricing to produce the welfare improvement identified in @sec-theory,
premiums must reflect expected damages, which is the product of release probability
and conditional cleanup cost. It is not enough that insurers can predict which
facilities will release; they must also be able to price the severity of the release.
I establish that remediation costs follow the same observable gradients as release
probability, using payment ledger data from state underground storage tank trust
funds in Colorado, Louisiana, New Mexico, Pennsylvania, Tennessee, and Utah. Because
these states retained public trust funds throughout the sample period, the cost
relationships I estimate here are not contaminated by behavioral responses to
risk-based pricing in Texas.

@fig-cost-distribution shows the pooled log-scale distribution of realized cleanup
costs across the six states, deflated to 2023 dollars. The distribution spans more
than two orders of magnitude from the median to the largest events, reflecting wide
variation in contamination depth, extent, and site conditions.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cost-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Pooled Distribution of Realized Cleanup Costs per Confirmed Release."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Figures", "Figure_cost_distribution_pooled.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Across six state trust funds (Colorado, Louisiana, New Mexico,
Pennsylvania, Tennessee, Utah), deflated to 2023 dollars. Log-scale density with
vertical lines at the median (dashed red) and mean (dot-dash orange).
\end{flushleft}

I estimate the relationship between realized cleanup costs and observable facility
characteristics using the following specification:

$$
\log(\text{Cost}_{it}) = \sum_{b} \alpha_b \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{AgeBin}_{it} = b]
  + \mathbf{X}_{it}'\gamma + \mu_s + \delta_t + \varepsilon_{it}
$${#eq-claims-regression}

where $\text{Cost}_{it}$ is total realized remediation expenditure for facility $i$
in claim-year $t$, deflated to 2023 dollars; $\mathbf{1}[\text{AgeBin}_{it} = b]$
are indicators for the facility's mean tank age bin at the time of the confirmed
release, with the youngest bin (0--5 years) as the omitted category;
$\mathbf{X}_{it}$ is a vector of facility-level controls comprising active tank
count, total tank capacity in thousands of gallons, and the facility's single-wall
share measured as a tercile indicator (0--33\% as baseline, 34--66\%, 67--100\%,
and an unknown category absorbing New Mexico facilities where wall type is
unrecorded); $\mu_s$ are state fixed effects; $\delta_t$ are year fixed effects;
and $\varepsilon_{it}$ is an idiosyncratic error term. Standard errors are clustered
at the state level. The sample is all confirmed releases with positive total cost
across the six control-state trust fund ledgers.

@tbl-claims-regression reports the results across four specifications. Column (1)
regresses log cost on the age bin indicators alone. The coefficients rise
monotonically: each older age bin is associated with larger cleanup costs relative
to the youngest-tank baseline. Column (2) adds the facility-level controls. The
single-wall share tercile indicators show a dose-response pattern, with facilities
in the highest single-wall tercile facing the largest cost penalty. Column (3) adds
state fixed effects and Column (4) adds year fixed effects. The age gradient and
the single-wall cost penalty both survive with minimal change, which rules out
cross-state administrative differences or secular trends in remediation prices as
explanations.


Age and wall type therefore predict both the probability of a release and the cost
of cleaning it up. The characteristics that determine private insurance premiums
are the same characteristics that drive actual environmental liability. Texas and
the control states entered the reform with different distributions of these
characteristics, which means a valid comparison group must match on these
dimensions.


```{r}
#| label: tbl-claims-regression
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive Correlates of Realized Cleanup Costs."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "Table_Claims_Regression_Main.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  lines <- readLines(table_path)
  lines <- lines[!grepl(
    "^\\s*\\\\begin\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\end\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\centering|^\\s*\\\\begingroup|^\\s*\\\\endgroup|^\\s*\\\\par\\\\endgroup",
    lines
  )]
  cat("\\scalebox{0.85}{\n")
  cat("\\begin{minipage}{1.18\\linewidth}\n")
  cat("\\begingroup\n")
  cat("\\fontsize{9}{11}\\selectfont\n")
  cat(lines, sep = "\n")
  cat("\\vspace{0.2cm}\n")
  cat("\\par\\footnotesize\n")
  cat("\\textit{Notes:} Dependent variable: log realized cleanup cost (2023 USD),\n")
  cat("per \\autoref{eq-claims-regression}. Column (1): age bin indicators only.\n")
  cat("Column (2): adds active tank count, total capacity, and single-wall share\n")
  cat("tercile. Column (3): adds state fixed effects. Column (4): adds state and\n")
  cat("year fixed effects. Standard errors clustered at the state level. Sample:\n")
  cat("confirmed releases with positive total cost across six control-state trust\n")
  cat("funds (CO, LA, NM, PA, TN, UT).\n")
  cat("\\endgroup\n")
  cat("\\end{minipage}\n")
  cat("}\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Claims regression table not found. Run 05\\_Claims\\_Analysis.r.}")
}
```



# The Make-Model Comparison Sample {#sec-sample-design}

## The Composition Problem {#sec-composition}

Texas and the control states entered the treatment window with different 
capital stock compositions on the two dimensions that predict environmental 
liability. Panel A of @tbl-descriptive shows that the average Texas tank 
was 15.8 years old at the reform date versus 15.0 years in the control 
states, a gap of $+0.8$ years ($p < 0.01$). The state-level single-wall 
share was 86.7\% in Texas versus 79.2\% in the control states, a gap of 
$+7.5$ percentage points ($p < 0.01$). These differences matter because 
age and wall type are not neutral characteristics. @fig-cell-risk shows 
that the predicted first-release rate for single-wall tanks rises from 5.3 
per 1,000 facility-years at age 0 to 2 years to 12.3 per 1,000 at age 24 
and above, while double-wall tanks remain flat between 3.2 and 6.4 across 
all age bins. The gap between wall types widens sharply after age 9. Texas 
therefore entered 1999 with a fleet that was both older and more 
single-walled than the control states, concentrated in exactly the cells 
where the predicted release hazard is steepest and where the private 
insurance premium gradient is largest. A pooled comparison that averages 
across these compositionally different populations would partly recover 
pre-existing differences in environmental liability rather than the 
behavioral response to risk-based pricing.



## Constructing the Matched Sample {#sec-cells}

I address the composition problem using Coarsened Exact Matching (CEM) on 
the four physical characteristics that determine a tank's actuarial rate 
cell: wall type, fuel type, capacity bin, and installation cohort. Tanks 
are matched exactly within bins on all four dimensions simultaneously. Any 
tank with no counterpart in the same bin on the opposite side of the 
treatment boundary is dropped from the estimation sample before any 
regression is run. This enforces covariate support overlap by construction 
rather than by regression adjustment, and ensures the treatment effect is 
estimated entirely from comparisons between tanks with close observable 
analogs.

The primary estimation sample restricts to tanks installed between 1989 
and 1997. This cohort window excludes tanks subject to the federal upgrade 
mandate, whose pre-1989 compliance-driven closures are unrelated to 
insurance pricing, and tanks installed after the reform, whose entry 
decision may reflect the new pricing regime. The restriction ensures the 
pre-period is free of mandate-driven closures and that each tank in the 
sample has an observed spell spanning both sides of the reform date.

Tanks with unknown wall type or missing capacity are excluded, as these 
cannot be assigned to a rate cell and their behavioral response is not 
governed by the same premium incentives the design targets. Cell-by-year 
fixed effect estimates on the full unmatched sample are reported alongside 
the CEM estimates throughout as a robustness check.


## Why Tank-Level Measurement

The closure decision is made at the facility level: an owner decides 
whether to retire a tank from a portfolio of assets. The analysis measures 
this decision at the tank level because tank-level data identify exactly 
which physical assets are retired and when, and because the Cox 
proportional hazard model requires individual tank spells with exact 
installation and closure dates. The facility-level pattern is recoverable 
from the tank-level estimates by aggregation, and the two levels of 
analysis are consistent throughout.



## Pre-Treatment Balance {#sec-balance}

@tbl-balance documents pre-reform balance across three progressively 
narrower comparison groups. In the full naive sample, the pre-reform 
closure rate is 10.16\% for Texas and 10.09\% for control states, a 
difference of $+0.07$ percentage points. The pooled make-model sample 
produces the same raw rates. Once the comparison is reweighted to reflect 
within-cell-year differences, the closure rate rises to 13.47\% for Texas 
and 13.88\% for controls, with the difference shifting to $-0.42$ 
percentage points and the sign reversing. The sign reversal confirms that 
the raw pooled comparison masks compositional differences: Texas facilities 
are concentrated in older, higher-risk cells that have higher baseline 
closure rates in both groups. The cell-weighted comparison removes this 
compositional confound and produces the quantity the estimator identifies.


```{r}
#| label: tbl-balance
#| tbl-cap: "Pre-Reform Balance: Full Sample, Make-Model Pooled, and Cell-Weighted."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "Table1_Balance.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  lines <- readLines(table_path)
  lines <- lines[!grepl(
    "^\\s*\\\\begin\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\end\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\centering|^\\s*\\\\begingroup|^\\s*\\\\endgroup",
    lines
  )]
  cat("\\scalebox{0.95}{\n")
  cat("\\begin{minipage}{1.05\\linewidth}\n")
  cat("\\begingroup\n")
  cat(lines, sep = "\n")
  cat("\\vspace{0.2cm}\n")
  cat("\\par\\footnotesize\n")
  cat("\\textit{Notes:}\n")
  cat("Read top to bottom as a progressive narrowing of the comparison group.\n")
  cat("Full Sample (Naive) is the unconditional pre-reform difference across all\n")
  cat("analysis facilities. MM Sample (Pooled) restricts to overlap-cell facilities\n")
  cat("but computes raw pooled means. MM Sample (Cell-Weighted) is the\n")
  cat("cell-size-weighted mean of within-cell rates.\n")
  cat("Shrinkage in the Diff.\\ column from Naive to Cell-Weighted measures how\n")
  cat("much of the raw TX/CTL gap is attributable to compositional sorting the\n")
  cat("design removes.\n")
  cat("$N$ counts are unique facilities observed in at least one pre-reform year\n")
  cat("(1990 to 1998).\n")
  cat("\\endgroup\n")
  cat("\\end{minipage}\n")
  cat("}\n")
}
```


# Empirical Framework {#sec-empirics}

## Estimation Strategy {#sec-estimation}

The analysis estimates the effect of the reform on the closure hazard of 
individual tanks using both a Cox proportional hazard model and an OLS 
linear probability model.


### Tank-Level Closure: Cox Proportional Hazard (Primary)

The primary specification for tank closure is a stratified Cox proportional 
hazard model estimated on exact-date data:

$$
h_i(t) = h_{0,c(i)}(t) \cdot \exp\!\left(\beta \, D_{it}\right)
$${#eq-cox}

where $h_i(t)$ is the instantaneous closure hazard for tank $i$ at calendar 
time $t$, $h_{0,c(i)}(t)$ is a cell-specific baseline hazard, and
$D_{it} = \text{Texas}_i \times \mathbf{1}[t \geq \text{Dec 22, 1998}]$ is 
the treatment indicator.Each combination of wall type, fuel type, capacity bin, and installation 
year defines a comparison cell that receives its own nonparametric baseline 
hazard through stratification, with no restriction on how that hazard 
evolves over time. These cells are narrow by design: matching on 
installation year within wall type and capacity means the baseline hazard 
absorbs any common aging trend among tanks of the same physical 
characteristics and vintage.

The treatment indicator switches on at the exact statutory reform date. Each 
tank's counting-process interval is split at December 22, 1998, producing at 
most two records: a pre-reform interval from installation to the reform date 
(or closure, if earlier) and a post-reform interval from the reform date to 
closure (or the end of the study window). All tanks have $D_{it} = 0$ in the 
pre-reform interval. Texas tanks have $D_{it} = 1$ in the post-reform 
interval; control-state tanks remain at zero throughout.

The coefficient $\beta$ is a log-hazard ratio. $\exp(\beta)$ gives the 
proportional change in the closure hazard for Texas tanks relative to 
control-state tanks of the same type after the reform.

The Cox model is preferred over OLS for three reasons. First, it conditions 
on survival to time $t$. If the reform causes high-risk Texas tanks to close 
early, the surviving Texas tanks in later years are a selected lower-risk 
subset. OLS averages over this changing composition and attenuates toward 
zero in long post-periods. The Cox hazard is estimated at each point in time 
among tanks still at risk. Second, it uses exact installation and closure 
dates rather than annual bins, eliminating measurement error from 
aggregation. Third, the stratified baseline hazard is more flexible than 
cell-by-year fixed effects: each cell receives its own arbitrary time 
profile, whereas cell-by-year fixed effects impose a common within-cell 
intercept shift at annual frequency.

The identifying assumption is that, within each matched cell, Texas and 
control-state tanks would have experienced parallel closure hazards in the 
absence of the reform. Consider all single-walled gasoline tanks with 
capacity between 5,000 and 12,000 gallons installed in 1992. The assumption 
requires that these tanks in Texas and these tanks in the control states 
would have been closed at similar rates over time had the insurance reform 
not occurred. The stratification ensures that any common trend among that 
group is absorbed into the baseline hazard, and $\beta$ is identified only 
from the divergence between Texas and control states within the group after 
December 1998.


### Tank-Level Closure: OLS Reference Specification

The OLS linear probability model serves as a transparent reference and is 
directly comparable to the prior literature on capital retirement. The 
estimating equation on the annual tank-year panel is:

$$
Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \gamma_{c(i),t} + \beta \, D_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$${#eq-ols-tank}

where $Y_{it}$ is a binary closure indicator for tank $i$ in year $t$, 
$\alpha_i$ is a tank fixed effect, $\gamma_{c(i),t}$ is the tank's 
make-model cell interacted with calendar year, and $D_{it}$ is the 
treatment indicator defined above. Standard errors are clustered at the 
state level.

The identifying assumption is the same as for the Cox specification: 
parallel trends in the annual closure probability within matched cells. The 
OLS estimate captures the average within-cell change in the annual closure 
probability for Texas tanks relative to control-state tanks following the 
reform. This estimate attenuates in the presence of differential 
survivorship, which is why the Cox model is the primary specification.


### Sample Construction and Matching

Both specifications are estimated on the CEM matched sample described in 
@sec-cells. The cell-by-year fixed effects estimates on the full unmatched 
sample are reported alongside the matched estimates as a robustness check; 
the two are in close agreement throughout.


### Tank-Level Event Study

The OLS event study replaces the scalar treatment indicator with 
year-specific interactions:

$$
Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \gamma_{c(i),t} + \sum_{k \neq -2} \beta_k \cdot
\mathbf{1}[K_{it} = k] \cdot \text{Texas}_i + \varepsilon_{it}
$${#eq-ols-es}

where $K_{it}$ is the calendar year relative to the reform. The reference 
period is $k = -2$, corresponding to 1997, two years before the reform took 
effect. Tails are pooled: years at or below $k = -5$ are collapsed into a 
single pre-period bin and years at or above $k = 10$ into a single 
post-period bin, which stabilizes estimates in years where not all cohorts 
contribute observations. The Cox event study follows the same structure, 
replacing the panel regression with year-specific interactions in the 
partial likelihood.


## Threats to Identification {#sec-threats}

### The Ideal Experiment

The ideal experiment randomly assigns the transition from community rating 
to experience rating across otherwise identical states and observes the 
same facilities under both regimes. Any post-reform difference in closure 
rates between treated and control states would then be attributable to the 
pricing regime change without qualification. Texas is the only state that 
made this transition during the study period, so identification rests on 
the contrast between Texas and the control states that retained flat-fee 
coverage. The make-model design, the matched sample restriction, and the 
diagnostics below are directed at showing that this contrast is internally 
valid.


### The Federal and Texas Upgrade Mandates {#sec-mandate-threats}

The primary threat to pre-reform parallel trends is a pair of overlapping 
regulatory mandates that generated compliance-driven closures before the 
reform window and affected Texas and the control states at different rates.

The federal EPA mandate under 40 CFR Part 280 required all tanks installed 
before December 1988 to be upgraded to double-walled construction or 
permanently removed, with compliance deadlines staggered by tank age across 
1989 to 1993. Texas simultaneously imposed an earlier and broader phased 
upgrade requirement under 30 TAC Chapter 334 that applied to a wider set of 
pre-existing tanks. The consequence is visible in @fig-full-closures: Texas 
closure rates run above control-state rates throughout the 1989 to 1993 
window, then converge once the compliance phase ends. Including pre-1989 
cohorts in the estimation sample would produce a spurious pre-reform 
divergence unrelated to insurance pricing.

The primary sample addresses this by restricting to tanks installed from 
1990 onward and excluding tanks that exit in their first year of operation. 
Tanks built after the federal standards took full effect entered service 
already meeting the new construction requirements and were never subject to 
the compliance deadlines that drove the pre-1993 closure wave. The 
first-year exclusion removes installation-year administrative corrections 
and failed-inspection removals that appear as closures in the raw data but 
do not reflect economic retirement decisions. @fig-ols-sample-closure-rates 
shows the raw annual closure rates for the matched estimation sample after 
both restrictions. The pre-reform Texas and control-state series track 
closely from 1993 through 1997, and the mandate-window divergence visible 
in @fig-full-closures is absent.
```{r}
#| label: fig-ols-sample-closure-rates
#| fig-cap: "Annual Closure Rates in the Matched Estimation Sample: Texas versus Control Group."
#| out-width: "85%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Output","Figures","Figure_OLS_Sample_ClosureRates.pdf"))
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Annual tank closure rates for Texas (orange) and the control 
group (blue) in the matched estimation sample, restricted to tanks installed 
from 1990 onward that survived their first year of operation. CEM weights 
applied. The shaded band marks the 1989 to 1993 mandate window. The dashed 
vertical line marks the reform date (January 1999). The two series track 
closely through the pre-reform window. The Texas series rises in 1998, one 
year before the statutory reform date, consistent with anticipation of the 
incoming premium schedule. Both series diverge after 1999.
\end{flushleft}


### Anticipation {#sec-anticipation}

The matched sample eliminates the mandate compliance problem but reveals a 
separate finding: Texas tanks show elevated closure rates in 1998, one full 
year before the statutory reform date. This elevation is visible in 
@fig-ols-sample-closure-rates and confirmed in the event study below: the 
coefficient at $k = -1$ (calendar year 1998) is $0.0041$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0004$) 
and significant at the one percent level, while all pre-period coefficients 
at $k = -5$ through $k = -3$ are flat and indistinguishable from zero.

The elevation cannot be attributed to the federal or Texas upgrade mandates. 
Tanks installed from 1990 onward were built to post-mandate construction 
standards from the outset and were never subject to the compliance deadlines 
that generated the pre-1993 closure wave. HB 3169 passed the Texas 
Legislature in June 1997 with an 18-month implementation window before the 
December 22, 1998 statutory date. Tank owners had over a year of advance 
notice that private insurance premiums would be set according to an 
actuarial schedule under which older single-walled tanks would face the 
steepest annual cost increases. Owners of such tanks had a direct financial 
incentive to close before the first premium assessment arrived.

The primary treatment indicator is dated to January 1999, which excludes 
1998 from the treatment window. This is conservative: it understates the 
total behavioral response to HB 3169 and treats the 1998 elevation as an 
explicit, visible feature of the event study. The reference period for all 
event studies is $k = -2$ (calendar year 1997), which precedes the 
anticipation window and provides a clean pre-reform baseline.


### Unobserved Heterogeneity

The residual threat is a time-varying shock to Texas that coincides with 
the 1999 reform and is unrelated to insurance pricing. Three pieces of 
evidence address this. First, the event study pre-period coefficients at 
$k = -5$ through $k = -3$ are flat across all specifications; a confounding 
shock would require an unobserved factor that left no pre-period trace and 
materialized exactly at 1999. Second, the treatment effect is concentrated 
among older tanks and single-walled tanks, where the theory predicts the 
largest premium increase. A general Texas economic shock would shift closure 
rates uniformly across all cell types. Third, tanks are physical assets 
fixed in the ground. A tank's treatment status is determined by where it 
was installed, not by any post-installation decision the owner can make. 
Owners cannot move tanks out of Texas to avoid the reform, and the reform 
applies to every tank in the state regardless of owner characteristics. 
Treatment assignment is therefore locked in and not subject to selective 
compliance or avoidance.

### Heterogeneity Identification

The main closure effect is identified within matched cells: Texas and 
control tanks of the same wall type, fuel type, capacity bin, and 
installation year are compared before and after the reform. The age 
heterogeneity result rests on a different comparison. Because installation 
year is part of the cell definition, all tanks within a stratum share the 
same age at the reform date. The interaction of the treatment indicator 
with above-median age at reform is therefore identified by comparing 
strata whose tanks were older in 1999 to strata whose tanks were younger, 
holding wall type, fuel type, and capacity fixed. This is a between-cohort 
comparison rather than a within-cell comparison, and it requires the 
additional assumption that older-vintage and newer-vintage strata are 
comparable except for their age at treatment. This assumption is plausible 
given that the comparison is restricted to tanks of the same physical type 
and capacity, but it is weaker than the identifying assumption for the 
main estimate. A finding of no age gradient would therefore be harder to 
interpret than a finding of no main effect.

### Reverse Causality

Reverse causality is not a concern. The reform date was set by the Texas 
Legislature through HB 3169 and implemented by the Texas Commission on 
Environmental Quality on a fixed statutory timeline. Individual facility 
decisions in the post-period did not affect the reform's existence or 
timing.


# Results {#sec-results}

## Tank Closure Duration {#sec-results-duration}

The transition to risk-based insurance raised the rate at which Texas tanks 
were retired relative to matched control-state tanks, and the response is 
concentrated among tanks that were old enough at the reform date to face 
the steepest actuarial premium increases.

### Baseline Estimates {#sec-baseline}

@fig-ols-sample-closure-rates documents the raw identifying variation. The 
Texas and control series track closely from 1993 through 1997. The Texas 
series rises in 1998 before the statutory reform date, then both series 
diverge visibly after 1999 with Texas consistently above the control group.

@fig-es-pooled-matched plots the event study coefficients from the pooled 
matched sample. Pre-reform coefficients at $k = -5$, $-4$, and $-3$ 
(calendar years 1994, 1995, and 1996) are $-0.0027$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0016$), 
$-0.0017$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0008$), and $-0.0001$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0006$) 
respectively, all indistinguishable from zero. The coefficient 
at $k = -1$ (calendar year 1998) is $0.0041$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0004$), 
reflecting the anticipation response documented in @sec-anticipation. From 
$k = 0$ onward the coefficients are positive in all years and statistically 
significant in most, with no systematic decay across the post-period.

```{r}
#| label: fig-es-pooled-matched
#| fig-cap: "Event Study: Effect of Reform on Annual Tank Closure Probability."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Output","Figures","F1_es_pooled_matched.pdf"))
```

\begin{minipage}{\linewidth}
\vspace{0.2cm}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} OLS event study coefficients from the pooled matched sample. 
Tank and cell-by-year fixed effects. CEM weights applied. Reference period 
is $k = -2$ (1997), marked by the open circle. The dashed vertical line 
marks the reform date (January 1999). The coefficient at $k = -1$ reflects 
anticipation of HB 3169 as documented in @sec-anticipation and is not a 
pre-trend violation. Pre-period coefficients at $k = -5$ through $k = -3$ 
are flat and indistinguishable from zero. Post-period coefficients are 
positive in all years with no systematic decay. 95 percent confidence 
intervals based on state-clustered standard errors.
\end{minipage}


@tbl-ols-stepwise reports OLS linear probability model estimates on the 
matched annual tank-year panel. Column (1) estimates the pooled DiD with 
tank and year fixed effects. The reform raised the annual closure 
probability for Texas tanks by $0.44$ percentage points (s.e.\ $= 0.0024$) 
relative to control tanks of the same type, a 42 percent increase over the 
post-reform control mean of 1.04 percent. Column (2) replaces year fixed 
effects with make-model-cell-by-year fixed effects; the estimate is $0.47$ 
percentage points (s.e.\ $= 0.0025$), confirming the result is not driven 
by cell-specific time trends. Columns (3) and (4) introduce the interaction 
of the DiD indicator with an above-median-age indicator, defined as tanks 
whose installation cohort places them at or above five years of age at the 
reform date. In both specifications the pooled DiD falls to $0.0012$ 
(s.e.\ $= 0.0022$) and is statistically indistinguishable from zero, while 
the interaction term is $0.0047$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0004$) and significant at the 
one percent level. The average effect in columns (1) and (2) is entirely 
attributable to tanks above the median age at reform. Columns (5) and (6) 
confirm this by splitting the matched sample: the DiD among younger tanks 
is $0.0027$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0023$), indistinguishable from zero, while the DiD 
among older tanks is $0.0052$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0025$), significant at the ten 
percent level.
```{r}
#| label: tbl-ols-stepwise
#| tbl-cap: "OLS Linear Probability Model: Effect of Reform on Annual Tank Closure."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "T1_ols_stepwise.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  lines <- readLines(table_path)
  lines <- lines[!grepl(
    "^\\s*\\\\begin\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\end\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\centering|^\\s*\\\\begingroup|\\\\endgroup",
    lines
  )]
  cat("\\scalebox{0.75}{\n")
  cat("\\begin{minipage}{1.39\\linewidth}\n")
  cat("\\begingroup\n")
  cat(lines, sep = "\n")
  cat("\n\\endgroup\n")
  cat("\\end{minipage}\n")
  cat("}\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Table not found.}")
}
```

@tbl-cox-stepwise reports Cox proportional hazard estimates on exact-date 
spell data.The strata are defined on the full matched cell: wall type, fuel type, 
capacity bin, and installation year. Age at the reform date is therefore 
fixed within each stratum. The interaction of the treatment indicator with 
above-median age at reform is identified by comparing strata whose tanks 
were older in 1999 to strata whose tanks were younger, within the same 
wall type, fuel type, and capacity group.
Column (1) gives a pooled hazard 
ratio of $1.251$ (s.e.\ $= 0.041$): on any given day after the reform, a 
surviving Texas tank faces a closure hazard 25.1 percent higher than a 
control tank of the same wall type, fuel type, and capacity. Column (2) 
adds the DiD-times-above-median-age interaction. The pooled DiD hazard 
ratio falls to $1.019$ (s.e.\ $= 0.049$) and is indistinguishable from 
one, while the interaction hazard ratio is $1.436$ (s.e.\ $= 0.084$), 
significant at the one percent level. Among tanks above the median age at 
the reform date, the daily closure hazard is 43.6 percent higher in Texas 
than in comparable control states after the reform. Columns (3) and (4) 
split the sample at the median: the below-median subsample produces a 
hazard ratio of $1.257$ (s.e.\ $= 0.065$), statistically 
indistinguishable from one at conventional levels, while the above-median 
subsample produces $1.240$ (s.e.\ $= 0.052$), significant at the ten 
percent level. The OLS attenuation relative to the Cox estimates is 
consistent with survivorship: as old Texas tanks close early, the surviving 
Texas sample progressively selects toward lower-risk units, compressing the 
annual closure probability gap.
```{r}
#| label: tbl-cox-stepwise
#| tbl-cap: "Cox Proportional Hazard Model: Effect of Reform on Tank Closure Duration."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "T2_cox_stepwise_01.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  lines <- readLines(table_path)
  lines <- lines[!grepl(
    "^\\s*\\\\begin\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\end\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\centering|^\\s*\\\\begingroup|\\\\endgroup",
    lines
  )]
  cat("\\scalebox{0.95}{\n")
  cat("\\begin{minipage}{1.05\\linewidth}\n")
  cat("\\begingroup\n")
  cat("\\fontsize{10}{12}\\selectfont\n")
  cat(lines, sep = "\n")
  cat("\n\\endgroup\n")
  cat("\\end{minipage}\n")
  cat("}\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Table not found.}")
}
```

# Discussion of Reduced-Form Results {#sec-discussion1}

The results establish two core findings. First, the transition from 
flat-fee public insurance to actuarially priced private coverage raised the 
annual closure probability for Texas tanks by $0.44$ percentage points 
(s.e.\ $= 0.0024$) relative to matched control-state tanks, a 42 percent 
increase over the post-reform control mean. The Cox specification yields a 
hazard ratio of $1.251$ (s.e.\ $= 0.041$), indicating that surviving Texas 
tanks closed at a 25 percent higher daily rate than comparable controls 
after the reform. Second, the effect is concentrated among tanks above 
median age at the reform date. Once the age interaction is introduced, the 
pooled coefficient falls to $0.0012$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0022$) and is 
indistinguishable from zero, while the interaction term implies a 44 
percent higher daily closure hazard for older Texas tanks (hazard ratio 
$= 1.436$, s.e.\ $= 0.084$). Tanks below median age show no statistically 
distinguishable response in either specification.

The age concentration of the response rules out a general Texas-specific 
economic shock as the explanation. A recession, a regulatory enforcement 
campaign, or any other state-level event unrelated to insurance pricing 
would shift closure rates uniformly across all tank types. The observed 
pattern is not uniform: it rises with tank age and is absent for younger 
tanks, which is the specific shape predicted by the premium-burden 
mechanism in @sec-theory. The private market charges higher premiums for 
older tanks, so the financial pressure to close is largest exactly where 
the observed response is largest.

The event study also documents anticipation. Texas tanks show elevated 
closure rates in 1998, one year before the statutory reform date. The 
event study coefficient at $k = -1$ is $0.0041$ (s.e.\ $= 0.0004$), while 
pre-period coefficients at $k = -5$ through $k = -3$ are flat and 
indistinguishable from zero. This pattern is consistent with 
forward-looking behavior: owners of tanks facing the steepest incoming 
premium increases closed before the first actuarial bill arrived. The 
primary estimates use the 1999 treatment date, which excludes this 
component and therefore understates the total behavioral response to HB 
3169.

Two caveats qualify the scope of these findings. First, the matched sample 
covers tanks installed from 1990 onward, which entered service already 
meeting post-mandate construction standards. The treatment effect for older 
pre-1988 tanks, which carry higher baseline risk and face steeper premium 
gradients, may differ in magnitude but cannot be estimated without the 
survivor-selection problem documented in @sec-mandate. Second, the Texas 
reform was a complete, overnight transition from public to private 
insurance. States considering incremental reforms, such as risk-adjusted 
surcharges within an existing fund structure, may see attenuated behavioral 
responses if the premium adjustment is partial or phased.

The behavioral response to this pricing change is consistent with the 
theoretical prediction that tying premiums to expected damages induces 
firms to internalize environmental risk [@shavell_liability_1984; 
@polinsky_strict_1980]. The mechanism operates through the cost of 
continued operation: risk-based pricing makes retaining an aging 
single-walled tank progressively more expensive, shifting the closure 
threshold in the direction predicted by @sec-theory.



# Dynamic Discrete Choice Model {#sec-structural}

The reduced-form estimates in @sec-results establish that risk-based pricing 
changes closure behavior. This section recovers the structural parameters 
governing *why* it changes behavior, decomposing the firm's response into a 
price channel and a risk internalization channel. The decomposition is 
necessary for counterfactual policy analysis: a mandate, a closure subsidy, 
and a Pigouvian tax operate through different channels, and the relative 
magnitudes of $\gamma_{\text{price}}$ and $\gamma_{\text{risk}}$ determine 
which instrument is most efficient.

## The Agent's Problem {#sec-agent}

I model a risk-neutral facility owner operating a single underground storage 
tank in an infinite-horizon discrete-time setting. Each period, the agent 
observes the facility state $x_t$ and a vector of idiosyncratic payoff 
shocks $\varepsilon_t$, then chooses $d_t \in \{\text{Maintain}, 
\text{Close}\}$ to maximize the expected present discounted value of future 
payoffs. The discount factor is $\beta = 0.95$.

## State Space and Transitions {#sec-states}

The observable state vector is $x_t = (A_t, w_t, \rho_t)$, where
$A_t \in \{1, \dots, 9\}$ is the tank's age discretized into five-year bins
(with $A = 9$ as an absorbing state for tanks older than 40 years),
$w_t \in \{\text{Single}, \text{Double}\}$ is the wall type (fixed over 
time), and $\rho_t \in \{\text{FF}, \text{RB}\}$ is the insurance regime. 
The state space contains $|\mathcal{X}| = 37$ states (36 transient plus 1 
absorbing).

If the agent chooses to close, the process terminates. If the agent chooses 
to maintain, wall type and regime persist while age evolves stochastically:

$$
A_{t+1} = \begin{cases} 
A_t + 1 & \text{with probability } \pi_{\text{up}}(A_t) \\
A_t & \text{with probability } 1 - \pi_{\text{up}}(A_t)
\end{cases}
$$

The stochastic aging process allows the model to match the empirical 
distribution of tank active life without imposing deterministic depreciation.

## Flow Payoffs and Behavioral Parameters {#sec-payoffs}

The core innovation of the structural specification is a three-parameter 
vector $\theta = (\kappa, \gamma_{\text{price}}, \gamma_{\text{risk}})$ 
that decomposes the facility's cost sensitivity into two distinct behavioral 
channels. Payoffs are normalized so that annual net revenue from operations 
is the numeraire ($\psi = 1$). All estimated parameters are therefore 
expressed in multiples of annual revenue.

If the agent chooses to maintain, the flow utility is:

$$
u(x_t, \text{Maintain}) = \underbrace{1}_{\text{Revenue}} + 
\underbrace{\gamma_{\text{price}} \cdot P(x_t)}_{\text{Premium response}} 
- \underbrace{\gamma_{\text{risk}} \cdot h(x_t) \cdot 
L(x_t)}_{\text{Liability internalization}}
$${#eq-flow-utility}

The premium response term captures the marginal (dis)utility of insurance 
premium payments, where $P(x_t)$ is the reconstructed premium from 
@sec-premium. In a standard neoclassical model, $\gamma_{\text{price}} = 
-1$. Estimates of $\gamma_{\text{price}} < -1$ indicate premium 
hypersensitivity, suggesting that explicit periodic insurance bills are more 
salient to owners than implicit costs, or that facilities face liquidity 
constraints making cash outflows particularly costly. Estimates where 
$-1 < \gamma_{\text{price}} < 0$ suggest inattention to fixed costs in 
closure decisions.

The liability internalization term captures the degree to which the facility 
internalizes expected environmental losses, the product of the leak hazard 
$h(x_t)$ estimated in the first-stage GRF models (@sec-risk-predict) and 
the expected remediation cost $L(x_t)$ estimated from the claims analysis 
(@sec-risk-costs). A value of $\gamma_{\text{risk}} = 1$ implies full risk 
neutrality and perfect internalization within the limits of the deductible. 
A value of $\gamma_{\text{risk}} < 1$ indicates risk myopia or 
undervaluation, where facilities discount low-probability high-severity 
events more heavily than actuarial models predict. This parameter is 
critical for policy design: if $\gamma_{\text{risk}}$ is low, 
liability-based regulations that rely on the threat of future costs will be 
less effective than ex-ante pricing mechanisms that operate through 
$\gamma_{\text{price}}$.

If the agent chooses to close, the flow payoff is the scrap value:

$$
u(x_t, \text{Close}) = \kappa
$$

The parameter $\kappa$ aggregates the liquidation value of the land and 
equipment, the opportunity cost of continued operation, and the avoidance 
of future fixed overhead, net of decommissioning costs. This value is 
assumed constant across states.

## The Value Function {#sec-value-fn}

Assuming the idiosyncratic shocks $\varepsilon_t$ follow a Type I Extreme 
Value distribution with scale parameter $\sigma$, the ex-ante value 
function is:

$$
V(x_t; \theta) = \sigma \log\left( \exp\left(\frac{v(x_t, 
\text{Maintain}; \theta)}{\sigma}\right) + \exp\left(\frac{v(x_t, 
\text{Close}; \theta)}{\sigma}\right) \right) + \sigma \gamma_E
$$

where $\gamma_E$ is Euler's constant and the choice-specific value 
functions are:

$$
v(x_t, \text{Maintain}; \theta) = u(x_t, \text{Maintain}) + \beta 
\sum_{x_{t+1}} V(x_{t+1}; \theta) \, F(x_{t+1} \mid x_t, \text{Maintain})
$$

$$
v(x_t, \text{Close}; \theta) = \kappa
$$

The conditional choice probability of closure is:

$$
P(\text{Close} \mid x_t; \theta) = \frac{\exp(\kappa / 
\sigma)}{\exp(v(x_t, \text{Maintain}; \theta) / \sigma) + \exp(\kappa / 
\sigma)}
$${#eq-ccp}

## Identification {#sec-structural-id}

The three parameters are jointly identified by exploiting orthogonal sources 
of variation in the observed conditional closure probabilities 
$P(\text{Close} \mid A, w, \rho)$.

The scrap value $\kappa$ is identified by the unconditional level of the 
closure rate across all states and regimes. In the optimal stopping 
framework, $\kappa$ acts as the reservation value: a higher average exit 
rate implies a higher relative scrap value, holding flow profits constant.

The price sensitivity $\gamma_{\text{price}}$ is identified by the 
differential closure response to the exogenous premium shock generated by 
the Texas regime transition. Consider two facilities with identical physical 
characteristics (same age $A$, same wall type $w$) but operating under 
different insurance regimes. The physical risk term $\gamma_{\text{risk}} 
\cdot h(x) \cdot L(x)$ cancels in the difference. The shift in closure 
probability across the regime boundary is driven by the premium differential 
$\gamma_{\text{price}} [P(A, \text{RB}) - P(A, \text{FF})]$, and because 
the premium shock is observed and exogenous, the magnitude of the behavioral 
response pins down $\gamma_{\text{price}}$.

The risk internalization parameter $\gamma_{\text{risk}}$ is identified by 
the gradient of closure rates with respect to the leak hazard $h(x_t)$, 
net of the premium channel. While premiums in the private market are 
correlated with risk, the correlation is imperfect due to deductible 
structures and coverage limits. If facilities face residual liability costs 
beyond the premium (deductibles, reputational costs, regulatory penalties), 
they exit high-risk states at a rate faster than premium costs alone would 
predict. This excess closure sensitivity to the engineering hazard rate, 
after controlling for the premium schedule, identifies 
$\gamma_{\text{risk}}$.

## Estimation {#sec-structural-est}

I estimate $\theta$ using the Nested Pseudo-Likelihood (NPL) algorithm of
@aguirregabiria_sequential_2007. The NPL procedure iterates between two 
steps. In the E-step, conditional choice probabilities are updated from the 
current parameter estimates using @eq-ccp. In the M-step, the structural 
parameters are re-estimated by maximum likelihood given the updated CCPs. I 
initialize the algorithm with the empirical CCPs computed directly from the 
data and iterate for $K = 3$ rounds, which Monte Carlo experiments on the 
simulation testbed confirm captures most finite-sample gains over $K = 1$ 
(Hotz-Miller); gains beyond $K = 3$ are small relative to computational 
cost.

The auxiliary inputs, the premium schedule $P(x_t)$, the leak hazard 
$h(x_t)$, and the expected remediation cost $L(x_t)$, are estimated in a 
first stage from the data sources described in @sec-risk-predict, 
@sec-risk-costs, and @sec-premium and enter the flow utility as fixed, 
pre-estimated objects. This two-step approach treats the environmental risk 
parameters as exogenous inputs rather than structural parameters to be 
estimated jointly, consistent with the principle that physical hazard rates 
are properties of the engineering system, not of the firm's optimization.

Standard errors are computed via facility-level block bootstrap ($B = 999$),
resampling facilities with replacement to preserve the within-facility 
correlation structure.

## Counterfactual Policy Scenarios {#sec-counterfactuals}

The estimated structural parameters enable comparison of four policy 
regimes, each holding the physical risk environment ($h(x_t)$, $L(x_t)$) 
fixed and varying only the policy instrument.

**Baseline.** The status quo estimated from the data ($\theta_{\text{base}} 
= \hat{\theta}$). This scenario recovers the equilibrium closure, leak risk, 
and welfare outcomes under the observed mix of flat-fee and risk-based 
pricing.

**Social optimum.** The Pigouvian benchmark in which the facility fully 
internalizes the health externality $E$ in addition to remediation costs 
$L$. Operationally, this scales the risk internalization parameter by the 
externality multiplier: $\gamma_{\text{risk}}' = \gamma_{\text{risk}} 
\times \xi_{\text{ext}}$, where $\xi_{\text{ext}} = (L + E) / L$ is 
calibrated from the health damage estimates of @marcus_going_2021.

**Closure subsidy.** A transfer payment that increases the scrap value by a 
fixed amount: $\kappa' = \kappa + s$, where $s$ represents a per-facility 
incentive payment funded by the regulator. This scenario tests whether 
reducing the private cost of exit can substitute for internalizing the 
social cost of continued operation.

**Mandate.** A command-and-control regulation that forces closure of all 
single-walled tanks exceeding a specified age threshold (e.g., $A \geq 30$ 
years and $w = \text{Single}$). This scenario tests the efficiency of 
targeted regulatory intervention relative to the price-based instruments.

The welfare comparison across these scenarios decomposes total welfare 
change into three components: the change in firm surplus (the private value 
of the option to continue operating), avoided external damages (the 
reduction in expected health externalities from accelerated closure), and 
government cost (the fiscal cost of the subsidy, if applicable). The net 
social welfare gain is the sum of these three components.


## Proof of Concept: Simulated Estimation and Counterfactuals {#sec-poc}

The results in this subsection are computed on simulated data generated 
from the model itself, not on the observed facility panel. The simulation 
calibrates the state space, transition matrices, and premium schedule to 
match the empirical moments of the Texas and control-state panels (tank age 
distribution, wall-type shares, pre-reform closure rates) but draws 
outcomes from the model's DGP with known parameter values. The purpose is 
to demonstrate that the estimation code recovers the true parameters, that 
the counterfactual machinery produces internally consistent welfare 
comparisons, and that the policy ranking is qualitatively interpretable. 
All point estimates, standard errors, and welfare magnitudes reported below 
will be replaced when the estimator is applied to the observed data with 
the first-stage inputs from @sec-risk-predict, @sec-risk-costs, and 
@sec-premium.

### Parameter Recovery

@tbl-structural-estimates reports the NPL estimates. The estimation code
recovers the three parameters from the simulated panel.
```{r}
#| label: tbl-structural-estimates
#| tbl-cap: "Structural Parameter Estimates (Simulated Data: Proof of Concept)."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

tibble::tribble(
  ~Parameter, ~Estimate, ~SE, ~Interpretation,
  "$\\hat{\\kappa}$",                "6.25",    "(0.84)", "Scrap value (annual revenue units)",
  "$\\hat{\\gamma}_{\\text{price}}$", "$-1.20$", "(0.31)", "Premium hypersensitivity",
  "$\\hat{\\gamma}_{\\text{risk}}$",  "$1.00$",  "(0.22)", "Near-perfect private internalization"
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(
    format = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    escape = FALSE,
    align = "lccc"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Estimates from the NPL algorithm (K = 3 iterations) applied to simulated data. All parameters expressed in multiples of annual net revenue. Standard errors from facility-level block bootstrap (B = 999).",
    general_title = "Notes:",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE,
    threeparttable = TRUE
  )
```

The scrap value $\hat{\kappa} = 6.25$ implies that the net liquidation 
value of a facility equals approximately 6.25 times annual operating 
revenue. The price sensitivity $\hat{\gamma}_{\text{price}} = -1.20$ 
confirms that the estimator detects the premium response channel: the 
magnitude exceeds unity, consistent with the premium hypersensitivity 
interpretation in which explicit, periodic insurance bills command greater 
behavioral weight than implicit or amortized costs. The risk 
internalization estimate $\hat{\gamma}_{\text{risk}} = 1.00$ implies 
near-perfect private internalization of expected environmental liability 
within the deductible. These values are the simulation's true parameters 
recovered by the estimator, not empirical findings about actual facility 
behavior. The qualitative patterns (price hypersensitivity, near-full risk 
internalization) are features of the calibrated DGP chosen to illustrate 
the model's ability to distinguish these channels.

### Closure Incentives Under Alternative Regimes

@fig-moral-hazard presents the predicted closure probability as a function 
of tank age under the two insurance regimes, holding wall type fixed.
```{r}
#| label: fig-moral-hazard
#| fig-cap: "Predicted Closure Probability by Insurance Regime (Simulated Data)."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Estimation_Results", "Moral_Hazard.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\par\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Simulated data. Predicted closure probability by tank age 
under uniform premium (gold) and risk-based pricing (blue). Under uniform 
pricing the closure probability is nearly flat in age, consistent with the 
zero premium elasticity prediction. Under risk-based pricing the closure 
probability rises with age, with the gradient steepening where the premium 
schedule imposes the largest marginal increases.

Under uniform premium pricing, the closure probability is nearly flat in 
age: the zero elasticity of the premium with respect to capital depreciation 
generates no age-dependent exit incentive. Under risk-based pricing, the 
closure probability rises with age, with the gradient steepening in the 
upper age bins where the premium schedule imposes the largest marginal 
increases. The gap between the two curves at each age bin is the 
model-implied treatment effect of the regime shift. This pattern mirrors 
the reduced-form heterogeneity documented in @sec-results.

### Policy Counterfactuals

@fig-policy-heterogeneity presents the change in closure probability 
relative to the baseline across the full state space under three 
counterfactual scenarios.
```{r}
#| label: fig-policy-heterogeneity
#| fig-cap: "Policy Impact Heterogeneity Across the State Space (Simulated Data)."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| out-height: "0.85\\textheight"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Estimation_Results", "Policy_Heterogeneity.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\par\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Simulated data. Change in closure probability (percentage 
points) relative to baseline across the state space $(A, w, \rho)$ under 
three counterfactual scenarios. The mandate targets old single-wall tanks. 
The subsidy shifts closure uniformly. The social optimum produces the 
largest and most heterogeneous effects.

Three patterns are visible. The mandate produces large closure increases 
concentrated among old single-wall tanks, with zero effect on double-wall 
tanks at any age. The closure subsidy produces a roughly uniform increase 
across the state space, consistent with an instrument that shifts the 
reservation value $\kappa$ without interacting with the risk profile. The 
social optimum produces the largest effects, increasing closure 
probabilities by 20 to 37 percentage points for single-wall tanks across 
all ages, reflecting the full internalization of both remediation costs and 
health externalities.

### Welfare Decomposition

@fig-welfare-decomposition and @tbl-welfare-cf present the welfare 
comparison across counterfactual scenarios.
```{r}
#| label: fig-welfare-decomposition
#| fig-cap: "Welfare Decomposition Across Policy Scenarios (Simulated Data)."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Estimation_Results", "Welfare_Decomposition.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\par\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Simulated data. Blue bars: change in firm surplus. Green 
bars: avoided external damages. Orange bars: government cost (subsidy 
fiscal expenditure). Dots: net social welfare gain.
```{r}
#| label: tbl-welfare-cf
#| tbl-cap: "Counterfactual Welfare Analysis (Simulated Data: Proof of Concept)."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

tibble::tribble(
  ~Scenario,         ~`Closure Rate`, ~`Leak Risk`, ~`Firm Surplus`, ~`Social Welfare`, ~`$\\Delta$ Welfare`,
  "Baseline",        "5.1\\%",          "17.93\\%",     "31.96",         "26.76",           "+0.000",
  "Social Optimum",  "37.3\\%",         "11.62\\%",     "29.98",         "26.67",           "$-$0.088",
  "Closure Subsidy", "7.8\\%",          "17.42\\%",     "31.54",         "26.48",           "$-$0.274",
  "Mandate",         "13.4\\%",         "15.40\\%",     "31.40",         "27.23",           "+0.467" 
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(
    format   = "latex",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    escape   = FALSE,
    align    = "lccccc"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Simulated data. ...",
    general_title = "Notes:",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE,
    threeparttable = TRUE
  )
```

On the simulated data, three patterns emerge. The social optimum achieves 
the largest reduction in leak risk but at the cost of a large reduction in 
firm surplus and a near-zero net welfare gain. The closure subsidy produces 
only modest improvements in leak risk and generates a negative net welfare 
change because the fiscal cost exceeds the avoided damages for the marginal 
facility induced to close. The mandate achieves a positive net welfare gain 
by targeting the subpopulation where the gap between private and social 
closure thresholds is largest: old single-wall tanks. The ranking of 
instruments on the simulated data depends on the structural decomposition: 
because $\hat{\gamma}_{\text{risk}} \approx 1$ in the simulated DGP, the 
welfare distortion is concentrated in the unpriced externality rather than 
in behavioral undervaluation of private risk. Whether this ranking holds on 
the observed data is an empirical question that the final estimation will 
answer.


## Limitations {#sec-limitations}

The structural model is subject to two principal limitations.

First, the model excludes the retrofit action. Without explicitly modeling 
the upgrade decision, the counterfactual analysis cannot simulate the 
impact of retrofit subsidies or technology mandates that target the 
intensive margin of capital improvement rather than the extensive margin of 
exit. The reduced-form evidence on the replacement margin (@sec-results) 
suggests this channel is quantitatively important. Extending the structural 
model to a three-action framework (maintain, retrofit, close) is a priority 
for future work.

Second, the estimates reported in this section are computed on simulated 
data calibrated to match the empirical moments of the Texas and 
control-state panels. The estimation on the observed facility-level panel, 
using the premium reconstruction from @sec-premium and the first-stage 
hazard and cost estimates from @sec-risk-predict and @sec-risk-costs, is in 
progress. The model specification, identification argument, estimation 
algorithm, and counterfactual framework are invariant to this substitution. 
Only the point estimates and welfare magnitudes will change.


# Discussion {#sec-discussion2}

The reduced-form and structural results jointly establish three findings 
that are grounded in observed data and one that is a proof of concept 
pending estimation on the observed panel.

The transition from flat-fee public insurance to actuarially priced private 
coverage raised the annual closure probability for Texas tanks by $0.44$ 
percentage points (s.e.\ $= 0.0024$) relative to matched control-state 
tanks, a 42 percent increase over the post-reform control mean. The effect 
is concentrated among tanks above median age at the reform date, where the 
Cox specification implies a 44 percent higher daily closure hazard 
(hazard ratio $= 1.436$, s.e.\ $= 0.084$), while tanks below median age 
show no distinguishable response. The event study documents anticipation: 
Texas tanks closed at elevated rates in 1998, one year before the statutory 
reform date, consistent with forward-looking behavior by owners facing the 
steepest incoming premium increases.

The concentration of the response among older tanks is the key 
identification result. The private market charges higher premiums for older 
tanks, so the financial pressure to close is largest exactly where the 
observed response is largest. A general Texas-specific shock, whether 
economic or regulatory, would shift closure rates uniformly across all tank 
types. The observed pattern, rising with tank age and absent for younger 
tanks, matches the specific shape predicted by the premium-burden mechanism 
in @sec-theory and is inconsistent with any confound that does not 
differentially affect old tanks relative to new tanks of the same physical 
type and capacity.

The structural proof of concept in @sec-structural demonstrates that the 
estimation code recovers the behavioral parameters from simulated data and 
that the counterfactual framework produces internally consistent welfare 
comparisons. The simulated estimates suggest that facilities are 
hypersensitive to explicit premium costs ($|\hat{\gamma}_{\text{price}}| > 
1$) while fully internalizing private expected losses 
($\hat{\gamma}_{\text{risk}} \approx 1$). If these patterns hold on the 
observed data, the welfare distortion from flat-fee pooling would be 
attributable to the unpriced health externality rather than to behavioral 
undervaluation of insured risk, with implications for the design of 
corrective instruments. These structural results are pending estimation on 
the observed panel and should be treated as illustrative until that 
estimation is complete.

Two caveats qualify the scope of the reduced-form findings. First, the 
matched sample covers tanks installed from 1990 onward, which entered 
service already meeting post-mandate construction standards. The treatment 
effect for older pre-1988 tanks, which carry higher baseline risk and face 
steeper premium gradients, may differ in magnitude but cannot be estimated 
without the survivor-selection problem documented in @sec-mandate. Second, 
the Texas reform was a complete, overnight transition from public to 
private insurance. States considering incremental reforms may see attenuated 
behavioral responses if the premium adjustment is partial or phased.

Thirty-eight states currently operate flat-fee UST trust funds. The Texas 
evidence shows that observable facility characteristics generate predictable 
variation in environmental risk that private insurers can price, and that 
firms respond to cost-based pricing in the direction the theory predicts. 
The mechanism operates through the cost of continued operation: risk-based 
pricing makes retaining an aging single-walled tank progressively more 
expensive, shifting the closure threshold in the direction predicted by 
@sec-theory.


# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This paper provides the first facility-level causal evidence on the 
behavioral response to transitioning from a flat-fee public UST insurance 
fund to a risk-rated private market. The argument proceeds in four steps. 
First, the institutional record establishes that Texas's 1999 transition 
was abrupt, driven by fiscal insolvency, and immediately absorbed by a 
private market whose premium schedule conditions directly on tank age and 
wall construction type. Second, the theory shows that this premium 
structure injects the facility's pollution hazard into its continuation 
value, generating a threshold-type closure rule that is absent under 
flat-fee pooling and that predicts a treatment effect concentrated among 
older, higher-risk tanks. Third, cross-validated risk models and the claims 
cost analysis confirm that the observable characteristics the private market 
prices genuinely predict both the probability and the severity of 
environmental releases. Fourth, the matched sample constructed on these 
same dimensions achieves pre-reform balance in a way the unmatched 
comparison does not, providing the identification the design requires.

The reduced-form estimates confirm the theoretical prediction. The 
transition to risk-based pricing raised annual tank closure rates by 42 
percent relative to matched control-state tanks, with the entire effect 
concentrated among tanks above median age at the reform date. Younger tanks 
show no response. The event study further documents anticipation: Texas 
tank owners closed at elevated rates in 1998, the year following 
legislative passage of HB 3169, before the first actuarial premium arrived. 
Both patterns, the age concentration and the anticipation, are signatures 
of the premium-burden mechanism and are inconsistent with a general 
Texas-specific economic shock.

The structural model provides a framework for decomposing the behavioral 
response into a price channel and a risk internalization channel, and for 
comparing the welfare consequences of alternative policy instruments. 
Estimation on the observed panel is in progress. The proof of concept on 
simulated data confirms that the estimation code recovers the behavioral 
parameters and that the counterfactual machinery produces internally 
consistent welfare comparisons. If the simulated patterns hold on the 
observed data, the welfare distortion from flat-fee pooling is attributable 
to the unpriced health externality rather than to firms undervaluing private 
risk, which would point to premium-channel instruments as the most 
effective corrective.

Thirty-eight states currently operate flat-fee UST trust funds. The Texas 
evidence shows that observable facility characteristics generate predictable 
variation in environmental risk that private insurers can price, and that 
tank owners respond to cost-based pricing in the direction the theory 
predicts. The policy question is not whether risk-based pricing changes 
behavior. The evidence here shows that it does. The open question is how 
much of the remaining externality wedge between private remediation costs 
and full social damages can be closed by insurance design alone, and how 
much requires complementary instruments. The structural estimation now 
underway is designed to answer that question.

\clearpage 


# References

::: {#refs}
:::

\clearpage



# Appendix B: Data Appendix

## Full Sample to Analysis Sample


```{r}
#| label: tbl-attrition
#| tbl-cap: "Sequential Attrition Log."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "TableB1_Attrition_Log.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  lines <- readLines(table_path)
  
  start <- which(grepl("\\\\begin\\{tabular\\}", lines))
  end   <- which(grepl("\\\\end\\{tabular\\}", lines))
  
  lines[start] <- paste0("\\resizebox{\\textwidth}{!}{%\n", lines[start])
  lines[end]   <- paste0(lines[end], "\n}")
  
  cat(lines, sep = "\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Table not found. Run 01l\\_DataQuality.R.}")
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm} \par \footnotesize \textit{Notes:} Each row applies one additional filter to the facility-year panel. $\Delta$ columns show the marginal change in facilities and facility-years at each step. The final analysis sample comprises 233,234 facilities and 3,967,583 facility-years.


### Balanced in Missingness



The statistical balance of data missingness is evaluated via a facility-level logistic regression model. Let $M_i$ denote a binary indicator equal to 1 if facility $i$ is excluded due to a missing tank installation or closure date, and 0 otherwise. The conditional probability of observing an incomplete administrative record is modeled as a function of treatment assignment, specified in equation (1):

$$
 \Pr(M_i = 1 | \text{Texas}_i) = \frac{\exp(\alpha + \beta \text{Texas}_i)}{1 + \exp(\alpha + \beta \text{Texas}_i)} 
$$

where $\text{Texas}_i$ is a dummy variable equal to 1 if facility $i$ is located in the treatment state, and 0 if located in a control state. The coefficient of interest, $\beta$, captures the log-odds of data attrition for treated facilities relative to controls. The maximum likelihood estimation yields a statistically insignificant coefficient on the treatment indicator ($p = 0.5361$). This result confirms the null hypothesis that $ \beta = 0 $, establishing that the probability of exclusion is orthogonal to the regulatory mandate and satisfying the requisite identifying assumption for the primary difference-in-differences estimator.



```{r}
#| label: tbl-missing-balance
#| tbl-cap: "Balance Test for the Missing-Data Exclusion."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis
#| 

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "TableB3_Missing_Date_Balance.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  cat(readLines(table_path), sep = "\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Table not found. Run 01\\_Descriptive\\_Analysis\\_REFACTORED.R.}")
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm} \par \footnotesize \textit{Notes:} The dependent variable equals
one if the facility was excluded from the estimation sample due to missing
tank-level date records. The coefficient on the Texas indicator tests whether the
exclusion rate differs between treated and control facilities. A statistically
insignificant coefficient ($p = 0.5361$) confirms that data attrition is unrelated
to treatment assignment.


## Data Quality by State


```{r}
#| label: tbl-data-quality
#| tbl-cap: "State-Level Data Completeness on Key Panel Dimensions."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis


table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "TableA0_DataQuality_ByState.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  lines <- readLines(table_path)
  lines <- lines[!grepl(
    "^\\s*\\\\begin\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\end\\{table\\}|^\\s*\\\\centering|^\\s*\\\\begingroup|\\\\endgroup",
    lines
  )]
  cat("\\centering\n")
  cat("\\scalebox{0.72}{\n")
  cat("\\begin{minipage}{1.39\\linewidth}\n")
  cat("\\begingroup\n")
  cat("\\fontsize{9}{11}\\selectfont\n")
  cat(lines, sep = "\n")
  cat("\n\\endgroup\n")
  cat("\\end{minipage}\n")
  cat("}\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Table not found. Run 01l\\_DataQuality.R.}")
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm} \par \footnotesize \textit{Notes:} Each cell reports the percentage of records missing the indicated field. Remainder states are excluded from the control group due to pervasive missingness on one or more dimensions required for panel construction. Washington and Oregon are excluded on institutional grounds , neither operated a public uniform-premium trust fund during the study window. ``---'' indicates the field is not applicable or the denominator is zero.


## Texas's Unique Federal Madate Upgrade Schedule

```{r}
#| label: tbl-mandate-schedule
#| tbl-cap: "Texas Phased UST Upgrade Mandate Schedule under 30 TAC Chapter 334."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

tibble::tribble(
  ~Vintage,                    ~Cohort,      ~`TX Deadline`,  ~`Yrs Before Federal`,
  "On or before 12/22/1964",   "Pre-1965",   "12/22/1989",    "9.0",
  "12/23/1964 -- 12/22/1974",  "1965--1974", "12/22/1990",    "8.0",
  "12/23/1974 -- 12/22/1979",  "1975--1979", "12/22/1991",    "7.0",
  "12/23/1979 -- 12/22/1984",  "1980--1984", "12/22/1992",    "6.0",
  "12/23/1984 -- 12/22/1988",  "1985--1988", "12/22/1993",    "5.0"
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(
    booktabs = TRUE,
    linesep  = "",
    align    = "llcc"
  ) |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Each row corresponds to an installation vintage cohort. The TX mandate deadline is the statutory date by which single-walled tanks in that cohort were required to be upgraded, replaced, or permanently closed. The final column reports the number of years by which the Texas deadline preceded the uniform federal deadline of December 22, 1998.",
    general_title = "Notes:",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE,
    threeparttable = TRUE
  )
```




```{r}
#| label: tbl-mandate-exposure
#| tbl-cap: "Mandate Exposure Comparison: Texas Pre-1988 Cohort versus Control States."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

table_path <- here::here("Output", "Tables", "Table2_Mandate_Exposure_Comparison.tex")
if (file.exists(table_path)) {
  cat(readLines(table_path), sep = "\n")
} else {
  cat("\\textit{Table not found. Run 01\\_Descriptive\\_Analysis\\_REFACTORED.R.}")
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm} \par \footnotesize \textit{Notes:} Comparison of cumulative closure rates and surviving fleet characteristics for Texas pre-1988 tank cohorts versus matched control-state counterparts. Texas cohorts faced staggered state-level compliance deadlines between 1989 and 1993, five to nine years ahead of the uniform federal deadline of December 22, 1998, producing differential survivor selection in the capital stock that enters the treatment window.




\clearpage

## State by State Claims Cost Distributions

@fig-cost-distribution-states presents the state-level distributions separately. The consistency of the heavy upper tail across all six states supports treating the skewed cost distribution as a structural feature of UST liability.
```{r}
#| label: fig-cost-distribution-states
#| fig-cap: "State-Level Distribution of Realized Cleanup Costs per Confirmed Release."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| out-height: "0.85\\textheight"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
fig_path <- here::here("Output", "Figures", "Figure_cost_distribution_by_state.png")
if (file.exists(fig_path)) {
  knitr::include_graphics(fig_path)
} else {
  cat(sprintf("Figure file not found at: %s", fig_path))
}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize
\textit{Notes:} Deflated to 2023 dollars. Each panel plots the log-scale density for one state with vertical lines at the state-specific median and mean. The consistency of the heavy upper tail across all six states supports treating the skewed cost distribution as a structural feature of UST liability.
\end{flushleft}

\clearpage
